content.problemStatement:
Given two integers AAA and BBB.


Let PPP denote a sequence of NNN prime numbers such that the sum of the sequence is AAA.
Let QQQ denote a sequence of MMM prime numbers such that the sum of the sequence is BBB.
Let XXX denote the **maximum absolute difference** between PiP_iPi​ and QjQ_jQj​ among all valid pairs (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) such that (1≤i≤N)(1 \leq i \leq N)(1≤i≤N) and (1≤j≤M)(1 \leq j \leq M)(1≤j≤M).
Find the **minimum** possible value of XXX over all possible sequences PPP and QQQ.

More formally, for all possible sequences PPP and QQQ, find the **minimum** value of max(∣Pi−Qj∣)\texttt{max}(|P_i - Q_j|)max(∣Pi​−Qj​∣), where (1≤i≤N)(1 \leq i \leq N)(1≤i≤N) and (1≤j≤M)(1 \leq j \leq M)(1≤j≤M).

Print −1-1−1 if any one of the sequences cannot be formed.

Note that, ∣X∣|X|∣X∣ denotes the absolute value of a number XXX. For example, ∣−4∣=4|-4| = 4∣−4∣=4 and ∣7∣=7|7| = 7∣7∣=7.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains two integers AAA and BBB.


Output Format
For each test case, find the **minimum** possible value of the **maximum absolute difference** between PiP_iPi​ and QjQ_jQj​ for (1≤i≤N)(1 \leq i \leq N)(1≤i≤N) and (1≤j≤M)(1 \leq j \leq M)(1≤j≤M). If any of the sequences can not be formed, print −1-1−1 instead.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers A and B.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case output a single integer on its own line:
- -1 if it is impossible to form at least one of the required prime sequences (i.e., A<2 or B<2).
- 0 if the minimum possible maximum absolute difference is 0.
- 1 otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: gcd(3,6)=3≥2, so both sums can be built using only prime 3 → answer 0.
Test case 2: gcd(3,2)=1, both numbers are ≥2, so we use primes {2,3} giving range 1 → answer 1.

Case 1: both are 2, common prime 2 → 0.
Case 2: gcd=1, need {2,3} → 1.
Case 3: A=1 cannot be expressed as sum of primes → -1.

solution.approach:
For a sequence of primes whose sum is X we may use any prime repeatedly. The smallest prime is 2 and the next one is 3, which differ by 1. Using only the primes 2 and 3 we can represent every integer X ≥ 2 (coin problem with denominations 2 and 3). Therefore, if both A and B are at least 2, a range of size 1 (primes {2,3}) is always feasible, giving an answer ≤ 1.

A range of size 0 is possible only when all primes used in both sequences are identical, i.e., there exists a prime p such that A and B are both multiples of p. This is equivalent to gcd(A, B) having a prime divisor, which is true exactly when gcd(A, B) ≥ 2.

Hence the answer for each test case is:
- -1 if A < 2 or B < 2 (no prime can be used).
- 0 if gcd(A, B) ≥ 2 (both sums can be built using only that common prime).
- 1 otherwise (use only 2 and 3).

The algorithm is O(1) per test case apart from computing the gcd, which is O(log min(A,B)).
